So what serializer method was for people that don't know is you could take a, I built this software where you would take a list of like, you'd have a market where there's a bunch of subniches. And I did it for the dog training industry. And that's how I sort of discovered it. So I built it where it would take all the dog breeds, and then it would go to Google's keyword suggestion tool. And if the dog breed was Labrador, it would say, okay, what are all the keywords people are looking for that are related to Labradors? And then it would put that in a folder, and it would do it for every single breed of dogs. And you could do it with anything, right? So like if you taught guitar, for example, you could get every single guitar manufacturer and get all the keywords related to that guitar, or every single guitar player's name, and get all the keywords related to that guitar player. So that's phase one of it. Phase two is it would then go to Google, and it would automatically create ad campaigns where it would have a campaign for Labradors, and it would have all the keywords for the Labrador retrievers in that campaign, in its own little ad set and everything. That affected conversion, I split tested them. The time between when they completed an order and when they saw the one click up sell. That was, do that matters, it affected conversion. So that's why I use click funnels over Cartra. Okay. Wade says, what agencies do search retargeting? Isn't it just Google ads? No, it's not Google ads. Search retargeting is interesting. It's not necessarily the search terms they type in Google. It's the search terms they type in any form, anywhere. So like WebMD, whatever. And the real magic isn't even necessarily search retargeting. It is contextual retargeting. So this is like we're dorking out now and I don't know if any self-serve places do this, but you find a bunch of keywords that people would be looking for and you do search term retargeting on those and you'll have a hit on those or not. And then you upload it as well into something called contextual search retargeting. And that means if the person is browsing web pages that have those keywords in them and they're doing it with a greater degree of frequency than average, then it'll start retargeting them because it means they're in market. And that's pretty gangster. It's pretty badass. So when we build search retargeting campaigns, we actually do both of those for people. We don't really explain it to them because it'll bore them, but that's what we're doing. So in the same offer from different webinar angles, at what point do you get diminishing return? Because I imagine you after a few different webinars, the only people left over are the ones really not interested in the offer. If that were true, that would suck.
